input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin system under study is a building within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, characterized by the integration of static structural models with real-time operational data for simulation, analysis, and optimization across the entire building lifecycle from design to operation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real-time monitoring of operational data, predictive analytics for maintenance and performance, energy and resource optimization, scenario simulation for design and refurbishment, and collaborative visualization tools, targeting AECO stakeholders including architects, engineers, facility managers, and construction teams to enhance decision-making and lifecycle management.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange, leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization, and is grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM) for structural and spatial data integration.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, maintaining live updates with minimal latency for operational monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Modular architecture with standardized extension points for component integration; DT Framework acts as a central hub orchestrating bidirectional data exchange among Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers for holistic building life cycle management
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability across heterogeneous systems addressed through nonfunctional requirements (NF_X), with standardized extension points enabling integration of custom services and analytics
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: BIM-based models enriched with sensors, actuators, and controllers to establish a unified semantic foundation; Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) extracts structured representations and automatically generates deployable Digital Twin artifacts with minimal manual intervention.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Validation and verification of model fidelity is performed using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric, which quantifies the alignment between the Digital Twin and its physical counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization of physical building data to their digital counterparts, enabling Facility Managers to monitor operations through the DT Framework.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Facility Managers interact with the deployed DT to issue control commands to physical building systems, supported by the DT Framework's bidirectional data exchange capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT Framework acts as a central hub for bidirectional data exchange, supported by a modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The framework features a modular architecture with standardized extension points for service integration, runtime configuration management enabling dynamic adjustments without full redeployment, and interactive visualization for immersive monitoring and control interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses device modeling within BIM environments by Building Control Engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers, forming a unified semantic foundation. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles extract structured representations and auto-generate deployable DT artifacts. Real-time data exchange synchronizes physical and digital counterparts, while interactive visualization enables monitoring and control. Modular architecture with standardized extension points supports evolution through custom services and analytics integration.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process employs Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) for automated artifact generation from enriched BIM models. Evolution is managed through a modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, enabling adaptation to operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Immersive interactive visualization interfaces support Facility Managers in monitoring system states and operating physical infrastructure via the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework addresses the absence of standardized integration frameworks for Digital Twins while ensuring compatibility with existing AECO-specific systems and standards such as BIM, which is the standard solution in the construction industry. It leverages BIM for semantic device modeling and structural representation, though challenges persist in standardizing dynamic DT components beyond static BIM data.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the domain (AECO) and the nature of the physical asset (a building), as well as the integration of static models with runtime data. However, it adds the unsupported word 'structural' to 'static models' and misses specific components like sensor configurations and control logic mentioned in the text. Supported by quotes: 'Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization'.

The extraction accurately synthesizes the various services described in the source, including monitoring, simulation, optimization, and visualization, while correctly identifying the target stakeholders and benefits. It is highly specific and well-grounded. Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities', 'rich, interactive visualization'.

Precisely lists the specific tools and frameworks mentioned for data exchange, visualization, and modeling in the source. Quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

The abstract states the framework 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange,' and Contribution R3 explicitly mentions 'Real-time data integration... enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins.' Additionally, the text mentions 'continuous synchronization' in Section III-A, aligning with the description.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. While the documents discuss Digital Twins in general and a specific Living Lab case, they do not describe multiple twin instances, hierarchical structures, or coordination mechanisms between twins for the specific system proposed.

The description is highly specific and comprehensively supported by the text. It correctly identifies the framework as a 'central hub' facilitating 'bidirectional data exchange', lists the specific roles ('Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers'), and mentions the goal of 'holistic building life cycle management'. It also accurately captures the 'modular architecture' for component integration.

The extraction is well-grounded in the text, correctly identifying 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' as being addressed by 'nonfunctional requirements (NF_X)'. It also accurately describes the integration mechanism using 'standardized extension points' for 'custom services and analytics'. It is specific to the system's approach to integration.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning:
The extracted characteristic accurately synthesizes the model-driven approach described in the text, specifically mentioning the use of enriched BIM models for semantic foundation and MDE for automatic artifact generation. This aligns perfectly with the framework description in Section III.A.

Quotes: 'enabling building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a unified semantic foundation', 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention'

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided source text focuses on the high-level model-driven framework and the *modeling* of components (e.g., defining actuators in BIM) rather than describing the specific physical characteristics of the actuators themselves, such as actuation ranges, limitations, or safety constraints. While 'lighting systems' and 'actuators' are mentioned as elements to be modeled, the detailed physical descriptions required by the characteristic are not present in the provided text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text discusses the modeling of sensors within the BIM environment and references sensor data in related projects (BOREALIS), but it does not provide specific details about the physical sensing infrastructure, such as sensor types, sampling frequencies, accuracy specifications, or spatial distribution. Therefore, the extraction is correct.

The description correctly identifies the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric as the method for validation. The source states: 'We demonstrate the practical application of our approach in a Living Lab case study and evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric.' It also notes the metric 'underscor[es] its interoperability and architectural soundness.' The extraction is accurate and directly supported.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The description accurately details the lifecycle from initial device modeling in BIM by engineers, through MDE-based artifact generation, to operational monitoring via visualization and future evolution. It captures the specific roles, technologies (BIM, MDE), and architectural features (modular, standardized extension points). Quotes: 'enabling building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations... and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts', 'framework’s modular architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the integration of custom services and analytics'.

The extraction correctly identifies Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) as the core methodology for generating artifacts and highlights the modular architecture for evolution. It is specific to the system described but could be expanded with more detail on specific validation or configuration steps. Quotes: 'model-driven framework... automates the extraction... and the generation of runtime interfaces', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'supports the integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs to evolve with changing operational requirements'.

The description precisely matches the text regarding the use of immersive visualization for Facility Managers to monitor and operate the infrastructure. It correctly links the technology (Unreal Engine implied by 'immersive interactive visualization') to the user role. Quotes: 'Facility Manager Monitors system states... operates the physical infrastructure via the DT', 'Unreal Engine... provides a rich visualization environment for interactive monitoring', 'interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control'.

The characteristic effectively summarizes the standardization context, identifying BIM as the industry standard and the framework's goal to address the lack of standardized integration frameworks. It also correctly notes the limitation of BIM regarding dynamic components. Evidence: 'absence of standardized integration frameworks', 'BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry', 'compatibility with existing, AECO-specific systems and standards such as BIM'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

block_6_judge.reasoning: The provided text focuses on the technical framework for model-driven deployment, BIM integration, and real-time data exchange. It does not contain information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy regulations, or consent management.

The source text describes the system architecture and components such as Eclipse Hono and Ditto for connectivity, but it does not detail cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or safety-critical fail-safe mechanisms.
